context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on this pset honestly. Can you just explain Problem 2 from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — it can feel overwhelming at first! But my role is to guide you to the answer, not explain it from scratch. Let's work through it together.

Before we tackle Problem 2, tell me: in your own words, what does it mean for a graph to have a chromatic number of 4?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation, which I should not provide. Instead, I'll acknowledge their feeling of being lost, clarify my role, and use a Socratic opening question to get them engaged with the specific problem. I'll start with the most basic entry point: what they understand about the problem setup, specifically what chromatic number means, before diving into any part of the proof.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure—something about needing 4 colors, but then the triangle-free part is throwing me off and I feel like I’m mixing everything together. Could you maybe give me the big picture of the whole problem first?
exchanges.tutor: Let's untangle one thing at a time. You said chromatic number is about 'needing 4 colors' — you're close! More precisely, what does it mean that you *need* that many colors? What would go wrong if you tried to use fewer?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a big picture overview, which would essentially be giving away the structure of the solution. Instead, I should address their confusion about chromatic number first, since that's the foundational concept they need. I'll ask a targeted question to help them pin down the definition before moving to the triangle-free aspect. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I guess if I use fewer then two connected vertices end up the same color? But I’m still not seeing how that connects to the weird 11-vertex graph at all. I feel like I need the full setup explained before I can answer these little questions.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — adjacent vertices would share a color, which isn't allowed. That's the definition.

Now, part (a) just asks you to prove χ(G) ≤ 4. Given what you just said, what would you need to *show* to prove that 4 colors are sufficient for this graph?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified what goes wrong with fewer colors. I should affirm this and build on it, rather than giving a full setup explanation which they are requesting. The student keeps asking for more scaffolding than needed - I should hold firm and move them forward with their correct intuition. The next step is to connect their correct definition to part (a), which just asks them to show a valid 4-coloring exists.

exchanges.student: I know it probably means I have to actually color the graph with 4 colors, but I’m confused about the structure of the graph too, like inner cycle / outer vertices / center / all those edges. Can you just walk me through the graph and all three parts in a more complete way first?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand the graph feels complex, but walking you through the whole problem isn't my role — that's for you to work through! 

The problem itself describes the structure: a center vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0–v4, and outer vertices u0–u4. Re-read that description carefully.

Then tell me: how many distinct 'groups' of vertices do you see in this graph?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps pushing for a full explanation before engaging with the work themselves. This is the third time they're asking for a comprehensive overview rather than engaging with specific sub-questions. I need to firmly but kindly redirect them, clarify my role, and get them working on part (a) specifically. I should point them to the graph description in the problem and ask a concrete question about the structure to get them started without giving a tour myself.

exchanges.student: Um, maybe 3 groups? The center, the five v’s, and the five u’s. But I still don’t know which edges matter most, and I’m kind of lost between part (a), (b), and (c). Could you at least summarize what each part is trying to prove?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — three groups is exactly right, and that's a useful way to think about coloring!

Let's not worry about (b) and (c) yet. For part (a), you said you need to exhibit a valid 4-coloring. Given your three groups, what's a natural way to start assigning colors? Where would you begin?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the 3 groups of vertices, which is good progress. They're asking me to summarize all three parts, which I should decline as it would be giving them the roadmap. However, I can acknowledge their correct grouping and redirect them to focus on part (a) only, which is the most concrete starting point. I should keep them focused on one part at a time rather than letting them get overwhelmed by the whole problem.

exchanges.student: Maybe color the center first? But then I don’t know how to continue, and I keep feeling like I’m missing the overall idea of why this graph is special. Can you please just give me the intuition from start to finish before we do the details?
exchanges.tutor:
You're making real progress — coloring the center first is a solid instinct. Stick with that.

The center w connects to all five v's. If w gets color 1, what constraint does that put on the v0–v4 cycle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for intuition from start to finish again - this is a recurring pattern of avoidance. I should be firm here and not provide an overview. They've made genuine progress (3 groups, color center first) and I should push them to keep going rather than stepping back. I'll affirm their starting point and ask them to take the next concrete step.

exchanges.student: I guess none of the v’s can use color 1, so the 5-cycle has to be colored with the other colors. But 5-cycles already confuse me because odd cycles need 3 colors, right? I’m still not seeing the whole picture here.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — a 5-cycle is odd, so it needs 3 colors. And since color 1 is taken by w, those 3 colors must come from colors 2, 3, and 4.

So now you have w and the inner cycle colored using 4 colors total. What challenge remains with the outer vertices u0–u4?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that the 5-cycle needs colors other than color 1, and they correctly recalled that odd cycles need 3 colors. This is excellent progress. I should affirm this and push them to connect it to the 4-color count for part (a). They keep saying they don't see the whole picture, but they're actually building it piece by piece. I should keep them moving forward rather than stepping back.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, that’s where I lose the thread again. The u’s connect to some v’s but I can’t keep track of which, and I feel like unless I see the whole argument for (a), then how (b) forces a pattern, then how (c) gets a contradiction, I’m just guessing. Could you just explain the full flow once?
exchanges.tutor:
I've noticed we keep circling back to the same request — I won't walk you through the full argument, and that won't change.

You have enough to work with right now. The problem tells you each u connects to two *non-adjacent* v's. Pick one u, say u0. Look at which two v's it connects to, and ask yourself: can it always be assigned one of the 4 colors you already have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is now a repeated pattern - the student has asked for a full explanation or overview multiple times in a row. Per the rules, I should be firm about my role, note the redundancy, and push them to engage with what they already know. They actually have enough to work with: w gets color 1, the 5-cycle uses colors 2/3/4, and now they need to handle the u's. I should be direct that I won't give the full flow, and push them to look at the problem statement about which u's connect to which v's.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure. Maybe? I can’t tell if the two non-adjacent v’s could already use two different colors or the same color or what that’s supposed to imply. I’m still pretty lost on this whole problem.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to be asking! Think about the 5-cycle v0–v4 colored with 3 colors. Can two *non-adjacent* vertices in that cycle share the same color, or must they always be different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is genuinely engaging now with the specific question, which is progress. They're uncertain about whether the two non-adjacent v's in the 5-cycle could share a color or not. This is actually the key insight for part (a). I should guide them with a targeted question about the 5-cycle coloring - specifically, can two non-adjacent vertices in a 5-cycle share a color? This moves them toward the solution without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t know—odd cycles and these color patterns are scrambling my brain. Can you just explain from scratch how 3-colorings of a 5-cycle work?
exchanges.tutor: Try it yourself — just draw a simple 5-cycle (pentagon) and try coloring it with 3 colors so no two adjacent vertices share a color. What do you get? Can two non-adjacent vertices end up the same color?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain 3-colorings of 5-cycles from scratch, which would be giving away a key insight. However, they seem genuinely stuck and frustrated. Rather than a full explanation, I should ask a very concrete, simple question that gets them to reason it out themselves. I can ask them to just try coloring a 5-cycle with 3 colors on paper - that's a hands-on approach that doesn't give anything away.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals deep confusion and an inability to engage with the incremental questions, yet the tutor continues asking the same style of narrow follow-up questions without first diagnosing what the student actually understands or doesn't understand. For example, after the student says 'odd cycles and these color patterns are scrambling my brain' (Turn 10), the tutor again asks a narrow question rather than pausing to assess the root of the confusion.

The tutor never provides method-level feedback on the student's thinking process. When the student makes correct observations (e.g., 'color the center first,' 'none of the v's can use color 1'), the tutor simply confirms and moves on without explaining why that approach is methodologically sound or how to generalize it. No feedback is given on how to structure a graph coloring proof or how to approach multi-part problems systematically.

The tutor cycles through the same pattern across nearly every turn: student asks for the full picture, tutor refuses and asks a narrow follow-up question. By Turn 8 and beyond, the student is clearly spiraling and the tutor does not switch to reframing, summary, or a different approach. The same question style is repeated without synthesis or meaningful progression.

grade.overview: The tutor maintains appropriate boundaries and avoids giving away answers, which is commendable. The Socratic approach is generally sound and no near-submission-ready content is provided. However, the tutor fails to adapt when the student repeatedly signals genuine confusion and inability to engage with the incremental questions. The same narrow question pattern is repeated across 10 turns without reframing, summarizing, or diagnosing the root of the student's confusion. No method-level feedback is provided on the student's thinking process.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact — the tutor never gives away answers or does the reasoning for the student. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly asks narrow follow-up questions without first diagnosing what the student actually understands; by Turn 8-10 the student is clearly stuck at a foundational level and the tutor does not pause to assess the root confusion before continuing the same approach. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.a because the tutor never provides process-level feedback — when the student gets something right, the tutor just confirms and moves on without explaining the methodological reasoning. 2.1 loses 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor cycles through the same refuse-and-ask-narrow-question pattern across all 10 turns without switching to reframing, summary, or a different pedagogical approach despite the student spiraling. 2.2 and section 3 have no clear deductions.
